title: How to burst the AI bubble: Strike at its roots
gist: Tech journalist Cory Doctorow argues the AI investment bubble—now $1.4 trillion in global CapEx against ~$50 billion revenue—is structurally worse than prior tech bubbles because every customer and every generation of AI loses more money. He predicts a ugly collapse that will nonetheless leave behind cheap GPUs, open-source models, and freed-up talent as a productive residue, while warning that AI companies are already jacking prices to clean up balance sheets ahead of IPOs.

themes.theme: AI bubble scale vs. prior tech bubbles

insights.claim: Global AI capital expenditure has reached $1.4 trillion—double what it was when Doctorow began writing his book—against sector revenue of roughly $50 billion per year, making AI the largest investment bubble in history by capitalization.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: The revenue-to-CapEx ratio is historically extreme; any mean-reversion or sentiment shift carries systemic risk to the 7 AI-heavy stocks representing over a third of total market cap.
insights.quote: When I wrote the book, capital expenditure (CapEx) globally was $700 billion, now it's $1.4 trillion. Meta wasted $60 billion on the metaverse. They spent $150 billion in the last three years on AI, and they say they're going to spend another $150 billion this year.

insights.claim: Unlike the web—where each new user made the business more profitable—every AI customer and every use of AI by that customer loses money for the AI company, and each new generation of AI loses more money than the last.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Negative unit economics that worsen with scale are a structural disqualifier for the 'it will eventually be profitable like Amazon' bull thesis.
insights.quote: AI is the money-losingest thing our species has ever done. We have never lost as much money as we've lost on AI.

insights.claim: Seven AI companies currently account for more than a third of the US stock market, and Doctorow characterizes their inter-company transactions as endlessly passing around the same $100 billion IOU.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Concentration risk: a bubble pop in AI would transmit index-level losses to passive investors and pension funds, not just tech-sector holders.
insights.quote: Seven AI companies currently account for more than a third of the stock market, and they endlessly pass around the same $100 billion IOU.

insights.claim: AI companies are transitioning from 90 percent price subsidies to 40 percent subsidies—more than doubling customer prices—in order to clean up balance sheets ahead of IPOs, which has already caused Uber's CTO to publicly question continued AI adoption.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: IPO-prep price hikes are an early leading indicator of demand destruction; watch enterprise AI renewal rates and public statements from CTO-level buyers at large consumers.
insights.quote: They're getting the bill because the AI companies are trying to get out before they're stuck holding the bag. They want to do IPOs, and to do IPOs, they need to clean up their balance sheet.

insights.claim: When the AI bubble bursts, GPUs will be available at pennies on the dollar, applied statisticians will be freed from large-company mandates, and open-source models will be available for optimization—analogous to cheap servers and developer talent after the dot-com bust that enabled Web 2.0.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Investors positioned in GPU secondary markets, open-source AI tooling, or lean AI startups post-bubble could capture outsized value, similar to infrastructure plays after 2001.
insights.quote: When AI bursts, you're going to be able to buy GPUs for pennies on the dollar. You're going to have your pick of applied statisticians, many of whom are very creative.

insights.claim: DeepSeek, spun out of a Chinese hedge fund with only $6 million in funding, produced a model running on commodity hardware so competitive that it triggered a $600 billion single-day market cap loss for AI incumbents—the largest 24-hour decapitalization of any firm in market history.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Efficiency breakthroughs from well-resourced but lean teams can disrupt the 'scale is moat' thesis overnight; capital-efficient open-source AI is an asymmetric threat to hyperscaler AI revenue.
insights.quote: When they launched, their model was so good running on commodity hardware that the market did a mass sell off, $600 billion in 24 hours—the largest 24-hour decapitalization of any firm in the history of markets.

insights.claim: Tech giants with 90%+ market share pivot to AI narratives because maturing stocks trade at a small fraction of growth-stock multiples, and growth stocks allow companies to acquire others by issuing shares—an advantage that disappears once they are reclassified as mature.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: The AI narrative is partly a financial engineering instrument to preserve growth-stock valuation multiples; scrutinize whether core business growth has stalled when evaluating AI conviction from Big Tech.
insights.quote: The capital markets have the object permanence of a toddler, and they would lose a game of peekaboo if they were drafted to play in the league.

insights.claim: Capital markets have historically accepted rapid pivots—from metaverse to crypto to Web3 to AI—with minimal penalty, provided the pivot is executed quickly enough, enabling serial bubble-inflation by the same actors.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Watch for the next narrative pivot when AI returns disappoint; the same companies will need a new story and the market will likely reward speed of reframing over substance.

insights.claim: Unlike prior productivity technologies (the web, PCs) which workers sought out and had to fight to bring into workplaces, AI is being mandated top-down by employers while many workers actively resist it, a reversal that signals low organic utility.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Bottom-up worker adoption is a historically reliable signal of genuine productivity gain; absence of this signal in AI is a red flag for ROI claims.
insights.quote: You look at those same press outlets today, and it's full of people saying, 'What are we going to do about the fact that no one in the workplace wants to use AI?'

insights.claim: Doctorow distinguishes 'centaur' workers (who choose how AI augments their work and report positive outcomes) from 'reverse centaur' workers (who are monitored by AI systems and serve as accountability sinks when AI makes errors), suggesting the same technology produces radically different outcomes depending on power dynamics.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: AI productivity claims should be evaluated by whether workers have agency over the tool; deployments that make workers accountability sinks likely produce liability risks and churn, not efficiency.

insights.claim: Amazon's cashierless Go stores were marketed as AI-powered autonomous checkout but were actually operated by teams of workers in India watching customers through ceiling cameras—illustrating that many high-profile AI demos mask human labor.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Scrutinize AI cost savings claims by verifying actual automation rates vs. hidden labor; vendor demos are unreliable signals of true AI capability.
insights.quote: There wasn't an AI. It was three people in India watching each customer through a network of cameras in the ceiling trying to guess what you put in your bag.

insights.claim: The 'jobspocalypse' narrative is financially motivated: AI companies need a story that converts $1.4 trillion in CapEx into equivalent revenue, and mass worker displacement by chatbots is the mechanism they are selling to enterprise buyers.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Job displacement framing is a sales tactic as much as a prediction; weight AI job-loss forecasts from companies with financial incentives to promote adoption with appropriate skepticism.
insights.quote: If you're trying to convince people that the way you're going to turn $1.4 trillion in CapEx into more than $1.4 trillion in revenue is by convincing bosses to fire workers and replace them with chatbots, you have to have a story about how the chatbot can do anyone's job.

insights.claim: Hollywood screenwriters and actors are the only US workers who successfully pushed back against AI because they are exempt from Taft-Hartley's prohibition on sectoral bargaining, allowing all workers in a sector to negotiate collectively with all employers—a lever unavailable to most American workers.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Legislative extension of sectoral bargaining rights is the single regulatory lever most likely to constrain AI labor displacement; track any legislative proposals on this front as a material risk to AI deployment ROI.
insights.quote: The only people who've ever beaten AI are the Hollywood screenwriters and actors. And the reason they were able to beat them is because uniquely, among workers in America, they are exempt from the Taft-Hartley Act's prohibition on what's called sectoral bargaining.

insights.claim: Copyright-based restrictions on AI training data are counterproductive because media companies will simply license their content to Big Tech in exchange for arrangements that harm individual creators, whereas labor law protections would benefit all workers directly.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Copyright reform is a weak protection for creators and a potential moat-builder for incumbents with deep licensing relationships; labor law reform is the higher-leverage intervention to watch.

themes.theme: Narrow AI use cases with genuine value

insights.claim: AI tools like Whisper running locally on commodity hardware without a GPU can provide full-text search and time-coded transcription across 30+ hours of audio, delivering practical value without large energy or data-privacy costs.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Local, low-cost AI inference on commodity hardware is a durable use case independent of bubble dynamics; edge AI and on-device inference companies may be more defensible than cloud-dependent foundation model providers.

insights.claim: The Human Rights Data Analysis Group is using GitHub Copilot to write software that surfaces arrest reports linguistically similar to cases that led to successful exonerations, demonstrating targeted AI utility in high-stakes legal contexts where precision matters more than breadth.
insights.speaker: Jennifer Ouellette
insights.so_what: Narrow, domain-specific AI applications with clear measurable outcomes are the sustainable use cases post-bubble; contrast with broad generalist deployments that struggle to prove ROI.
